1: The arrival at Sinai on the third month is the structural hinge of Exodus: everything before this is the journey from Egypt; everything after is the covenant at Sinai. Christ is the new covenant mediator (Hebrews 9:15; Exodus 12:24) toward whom this whole movement aimed — Israel was brought to the mountain so YHWH could dwell with them; Christ is the one through whom YHWH dwells in his people permanently (John 14:23; 1 Corinthians 3:16).
2: Israel camped in the wilderness opposite the mountain. The meeting at the mountain — YHWH descends, Israel assembles below — is the type of the Incarnation-encounter: YHWH descends in human flesh to the level of his people, and they are drawn to him (John 12:32). The gathering of the whole community reflects the eschatological gathering of all nations to the mountain of YHWH (Isaiah 2:2-3; Hebrews 12:22-24).
3: Moses went up to God; YHWH called to him from the mountain. The mediatorial ascent of Moses — going up into the divine presence on behalf of the people — is the type of Christ's ascension (Hebrews 9:24). Moses goes up to receive the covenant; Christ ascends to present his blood and secure the new covenant. Moses came down to the people; Christ sends the Spirit to his people (John 14:16-18; Acts 2).
4: “I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.” YHWH's drawing of Israel to himself is the type of the Father's drawing of people to the Son: “No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him” (John 6:44). The eagle-wings image (Deuteronomy 32:11; Psalms 36:7; Exodus 91:4) of divine protective transport is the type of the Spirit who carries believers into union with Christ (Galatians 5:25; Ephesians 2:5-6).
5: “You will be my treasured possession (segulah) out of all the nations.” The segulah-language is applied to the church in 1 Peter 2:9 (“a people for his own possession”) and Titus 2:14 (“a people of his own possession, zealous for good works”). Christ's death purchases for himself a people who are his treasured possession — the new Israel gathered from every nation (Revelation 5:9-10).
6: “You will be for me a kingdom of priests (mamlekhet kohanim) and a holy nation (goy kadosh).” Applied to the church in 1 Peter 2:9 (“a royal priesthood, a holy nation”) and Revelation 1:6; Exodus 5:10; Exodus 20:6 (“a kingdom of priests to our God”). Christ as both King and High Priest (Hebrews 7:1-3; Psalms 110:4) is himself the Melchizedekian fulfillment of both titles, and through him his people share both vocations.
7: Moses summons the elders and sets before them all YHWH's words. The role of Moses as covenant-transmitter — YHWH speaks to Moses, Moses to the elders, the elders to the people — is the type of the prophetic-apostolic chain: Christ speaks to the apostles, who speak to the church. Hebrews 1:1-2: “In these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.”
8: “All the people responded together: We will do everything YHWH has said.” The people's covenant commitment — made in good faith but unrealized in practice (the golden calf follows in Exodus 32) — is the OT demonstration of the need for a new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34). The inability to keep the Sinai covenant is the OT argument for Christ: Israel's repeated failure proves that human resolution cannot achieve covenant obedience; a new heart must be given (Ezekiel 36:26-27).
9: YHWH comes in a thick cloud so the people will hear him speaking with Moses. The cloud conceals YHWH from direct vision. The contrast with the Incarnation is sharp: in Christ, YHWH becomes visible, audible, and touchable — “what we have heard, what we have seen with our eyes, what we have looked at and touched with our hands” (1 John 1:1-3). Sinai's concealing cloud and the Incarnation's visible fullness form an instructive contrast in Hebrews (Hebrews 12:18-24).
10: Consecration before YHWH's appearance — washing clothes and preparing on the third day. The third-day timing is Christologically significant: YHWH descends on the third day; Christ is raised on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:4). The pattern of a three-day preparation before divine encounter on the third day runs from Exod 19 through the resurrection narrative. The washing-before-approach is the type of baptismal preparation (Hebrews 10:22; Titus 3:5).
11: “On the third day YHWH will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all the people.” The third-day descent of YHWH in fire and cloud is the most significant OT type of the resurrection-revelation: YHWH appears on the third day to the assembled community. Christ rises on the third day and appears to his community (1 Corinthians 15:5-8). Both events are the definitive public revelation of YHWH's power over death and covenant faithfulness.
12: The boundaries around the mountain — the people must not go up or touch it, on pain of death. Hebrews 12:18-19 cites these very boundaries as the terror of the old covenant: “the blazing fire and darkness and gloom and a tempest” from which the people shrank back. The contrast in Hebrews 12:22-24: “But you have come to Mount Zion” — through Christ's blood, the barriers are removed and bold approach is possible (Hebrews 10:19-22).
13: Even the animal that touches the mountain must die — stoned or shot with arrows. The death-consequence for unauthorized approach to divine holiness reflects the infinite distance between the holy God and sinful humanity. Christ is the one who crossed that boundary on our behalf — he bore the penalty of unauthorized approach (death) so that his people might have authorized, perpetual access (Ephesians 2:18; Hebrews 4:16).
14: Moses consecrates the people — they wash their clothes. The priestly act of preparation for divine encounter is performed by Moses as mediator. Christ himself is the one who washes and consecrates his people: “Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word” (Ephesians 5:25-26). The external washing of clothes is the type of the internal cleansing of conscience (Hebrews 9:14).
15: The instruction to abstain from sexual relations before the third day — ritual purity before meeting YHWH. Paul applies bodily purity language to the church as the temple of the Spirit: “Your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit” (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). The preparatory abstinence for Sinai is the type of the ongoing holiness required to live in the immediate presence of YHWH through the Spirit (2 Corinthians 6:14-7:1).
16: Thunder, lightning, thick cloud, and very loud trumpet blast on the third day — the people trembled. Hebrews 12:19-21 names the trumpet and the voice as among the things that caused Israel to beg that no further word be spoken. The terror of Sinai communicates the infinite holiness of YHWH; the new covenant's access through Christ does not abolish that holiness but provides a mediator equal to its demands (Hebrews 7:26-27).
17: Moses led the people out of the camp to meet with God — they stood at the foot of the mountain. The corporate assembly before YHWH is the type of the eschatological assembly before the throne (Revelation 7:9-12; Exodus 19:1-8). Moses leading the people to the meeting is the type of Christ leading his flock into the Father's presence (John 10:27-29; Hebrews 2:13: “Here am I, and the children God has given me”).
18: Mount Sinai covered in smoke because YHWH descended in fire — the smoke billowed up like smoke from a furnace. The Spirit descended at Pentecost as “tongues of fire” (Acts 2:3). The Sinai fire-theophany is the type of the Pentecost fire-theophany: both mark a covenant inauguration. At Sinai YHWH came in fire to give the law; at Pentecost the Spirit came in fire to write the law on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3-6).
19: The trumpet grew louder and louder; Moses spoke and God answered him. The loud trumpet at the covenant meeting is the type of the last trumpet (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Revelation 11:15) — YHWH's voice like a trumpet announcing covenant assembly prefigures the eschatological trumpet that announces the final gathering and the completion of all covenant promises.
20: YHWH descends to the top of Sinai and calls Moses up. The ascent-of-the-mediator-into-divine-presence is the type of Christ's ascension (Ephesians 4:8-10, citing Psalms 68:18: “When he ascended on high, he led captives in his train”). Moses goes up to receive the covenant; Christ ascends to present his blood and secure the eternal covenant (Hebrews 9:11-14). Moses descends to the people with the law; Christ sends the Spirit with new-covenant life.
21: YHWH warns that the people must not force their way through to see him, or many will perish. The prohibition on unauthorized approach is the negative context for Hebrews 10:19-22's invitation: “therefore, brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus.” The barrier Sinai erected, Christ's blood removes — not by lowering YHWH's holiness but by providing the perfect High Priest who has met its demands.
22: Even the priests who approach YHWH must consecrate themselves or he will break out against them. Christ is the high priest whose holiness is intrinsic, not derived (Hebrews 7:26-27: “holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners”). He needs no prior consecration because he is the source of all holiness. His self-consecration (John 17:19: “I consecrate myself”) enables the consecration of his people.
23: Moses's protest — the people cannot come up; YHWH warned us: “set boundaries around the mountain and consecrate it.” Moses intercedes on behalf of the people, clarifying the limits already established. The intercessory function of Moses before YHWH when the people's welfare is at stake is the type of Christ's perpetual intercession: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25).
24: YHWH's graduated access: Moses and Aaron may come up; the priests and people must not break through. The structure of graduated access at Sinai (Moses → Aaron → priests → people → none) is the old covenant's access structure. Christ dismantles the gradient: he is the single point of access, and through him all believers have equal boldness before the Father (Ephesians 2:18: “through him we both have access to the Father by one Spirit”).
25: Moses goes down and tells the people. The mediator's movement: up into the divine presence, down to the human community, back up again (v20, v25, then again in Exodus 24:18). The repeated ascents and descents of Moses sketch the pattern fulfilled in Christ's incarnation-crucifixion-resurrection-ascension: the complete movement of the divine mediator through every level of the cosmos for the sake of his people (Ephesians 4:9-10).
